title: Macronutrients, Sleep Science & Evaluating Claims
pillar_name: Physical Literacy, Martial Arts & Human Performance

suggested.title: [App/Tool] A food tracking app (MyFitnessPal or Cronometer) — used for 2 weeks only
[Video] YouTube: Jeff Nippard or Layne Norton evidence-based nutrition videos

competence_markers.description: The student can explain the three macronutrients, their caloric values, and their primary functions
The student can estimate their daily protein needs and identify whether their current diet meets them
The student can explain caloric balance and its role in body composition
The student can describe sleep architecture and explain why 8–10 hours matters for adolescents
The student can evaluate a nutrition claim using the BS Detector framework
The student practices sleep hygiene consistently — consistent schedule, no screens before bed, dark room
The student includes rest days in their training schedule and understands recovery as a practice

cross_connections.description: Evaluating nutrition claims uses the same critical thinking tools as evaluating political claims. The supplement industry is a case study in propaganda.
Macronutrient metabolism, energy systems, and sleep architecture are applied biology and chemistry.
The student who understands macronutrients should be learning to cook meals that meet their nutritional needs.
Nutritional discipline — consistent protein intake, sleep hygiene, hydration — is IC2 applied daily.

content.why_this_matters:
You have been eating real food, drinking water, and sleeping well. Those habits are the foundation. Now you need to understand why those habits work — and more importantly, you need to develop the ability to evaluate the tidal wave of nutritional claims, diet trends, and supplement marketing that will come at you for the rest of your life.

The nutrition and fitness industry is a multi-billion dollar industry built largely on selling people things they do not need, using claims that do not survive contact with rigorous evidence. Fat-burning supplements that do not burn fat. Diets that promise transformation but deliver temporary weight loss followed by rebound. Protein bars that are essentially candy bars with marketing. Sports drinks that are unnecessary for 95% of the people who buy them.

You are going to learn the actual science — what the body needs, how macronutrients work, why calories matter, and what the evidence says about sleep and recovery. Armed with this knowledge, you will be able to look at any nutritional claim and ask the right questions: What is the evidence? Who is selling this? Does the effect size actually matter? Is this based on human studies or rat studies? The same critical thinking tools you are building in 1.7 (Ideology Immunity) apply directly to nutrition claims — because the nutrition industry uses the same propaganda techniques as any other industry that profits from confusion.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Macronutrient literacy** — understanding protein, carbohydrates, and fat as the three categories of food energy
2. **The protein question** — how much protein a training adolescent actually needs, based on evidence
3. **Caloric balance** — the fundamental reality that body composition is determined by energy in versus energy out
4. **Sleep science** — sleep architecture, growth hormone release, and why 8–10 hours is a biological requirement
5. **Evaluating nutrition claims** — the critical thinking skills to navigate marketing and misinformation
6. **Hydration beyond water** — electrolytes and their role during extended training
7. **Recovery as a practice** — rest days, active recovery, and the principle that adaptation happens during recovery
content.core_content:
### Part 1: The Three Macronutrients

Every food you eat is made up of three categories of molecules that provide energy: protein, carbohydrates, and fat. These are the macronutrients — "macro" because your body needs them in large quantities, as opposed to micronutrients (vitamins and minerals) which you need in small quantities.

**Protein — 4 calories per gram**
Protein is the building material for muscle, bone, skin, enzymes, hormones, and immune cells. When you eat protein, your body breaks it down into amino acids and uses those amino acids to build and repair tissues. For a training person, protein is the most important macronutrient because it directly determines whether your body can recover from training and build new muscle.

The best sources of protein are animal-based: meat, fish, eggs, and dairy. These are "complete proteins" — they contain all the essential amino acids your body needs in the right proportions. Plant proteins (beans, lentils, tofu, grains) are generally "incomplete" — they are missing or low in one or more essential amino acids. Plant proteins can work, but you need to combine different plant sources to get a complete amino acid profile, and you typically need to eat more total food to get the same amount of usable protein.

**Carbohydrates — 4 calories per gram**
Carbohydrates are your body's primary fuel for high-intensity activity. When you sprint, wrestle, lift weights, or do anything explosive, your muscles are burning carbohydrates (stored as glycogen in the muscle). When you eat carbohydrates, your body breaks them down into glucose, which is used immediately for energy or stored as glycogen for later use.

Good sources: rice, potatoes, sweet potatoes, oats, fruit, vegetables. These are whole-food carbohydrates that come with fiber, vitamins, and minerals. Processed carbohydrates (white bread, candy, sugary cereals, soda) provide glucose without the accompanying nutrients and are absorbed so quickly that they spike blood sugar and promote fat storage when consumed in excess.

**Fat — 9 calories per gram**
Fat is essential for hormone production (including testosterone and growth hormone), cell membrane structure, brain function, and absorption of fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K). Fat is also the most energy-dense macronutrient — 9 calories per gram versus 4 for protein and carbohydrates.

Good sources: eggs, fatty fish (salmon, sardines), avocado, nuts, olive oil, butter, animal fats from quality sources. The fear of dietary fat that dominated nutrition advice from the 1970s through the 2000s was largely wrong and has been substantially revised. Healthy fats are essential, not dangerous.

### Part 2: The Protein Question — How Much Do You Actually Need?

This is the most practically important question in sports nutrition, and the answer is clear from the research: a physically active, growing adolescent needs approximately 0.7–1.0 grams of protein per pound of bodyweight per day.

Let us make that concrete. If you weigh 120 pounds, you need 84–120 grams of protein per day. What does that look like in food?

- 3 eggs at breakfast = ~18g protein
- Chicken breast at lunch = ~30g protein
- Greek yogurt as a snack = ~15g protein
- Steak or fish at dinner = ~35g protein
- Glass of milk = ~8g protein
- Total: ~106g protein

That is achievable with real food. No supplements required. But notice that it requires protein at every meal and a deliberate effort to include high-protein foods. Most teenagers eating a typical modern diet — cereal for breakfast, a sandwich for lunch, pasta for dinner — fall significantly short of this target.

**Why does protein matter so much for you specifically?** Because you are doing two things simultaneously that require protein: you are growing (building new tissue as part of normal development) and you are training (creating muscle damage that requires repair and adaptation). Both processes compete for the same resource. If protein intake is insufficient, your body will prioritize basic growth over training adaptation — meaning you will grow normally but your training results will be blunted.

### Part 3: Caloric Balance — The Master Equation

Body composition — how much of your body is muscle versus fat — is primarily determined by one equation: calories in versus calories out.

If you eat more calories than you burn, the excess is stored (mostly as fat). If you eat fewer calories than you burn, the deficit is covered by breaking down stored energy (ideally fat, but also some muscle if protein is insufficient). If you eat approximately what you burn, your weight stays stable.

This is not controversial in the scientific literature. It is thermodynamics. Every diet that has ever produced fat loss — low carb, low fat, intermittent fasting, keto, paleo, vegan — has done so by creating a caloric deficit. The mechanism by which the deficit is created varies, but the deficit itself is the cause.

**At your age, this is important context, not a prescription.** You should not be restricting calories. You are growing and training. You need to eat enough — probably more than you think. The caloric balance concept becomes practical when you are older and may want to adjust body composition intentionally. For now, eat real food, eat enough of it, prioritize protein, and let your body do what it needs to do.

### Part 4: Sleep Science — What Happens While You Are Unconscious

Sleep is not a passive state. It is the most metabolically active period of your day, organized into a structured cycle of distinct stages, each serving different functions.

**Sleep architecture:**
- **Light sleep (Stages 1–2):** Transition into sleep. Heart rate slows. Body temperature drops. This is the entry phase.
- **Deep sleep (Stages 3–4):** The physical recovery stage. Growth hormone is released primarily during deep sleep. Muscle repair happens here. Bone density increases here. Immune function is restored here. This is the stage that matters most for physical development.
- **REM sleep (Rapid Eye Movement):** The cognitive recovery stage. Memory consolidation — everything you learned that day is organized and stored. Emotional processing occurs here. This is the stage that matters most for learning and psychological health.

Your body cycles through these stages approximately every 90 minutes. A full night includes 4–6 complete cycles. Here is the critical detail: deep sleep is concentrated in the first half of the night, and REM sleep is concentrated in the second half. If you cut your sleep short by two hours, you are primarily losing REM sleep — which means you are losing the memory consolidation and emotional processing that the second half of the night provides.

**For adolescents, 8–10 hours is a biological requirement.** Your brain is undergoing massive reorganization during adolescence — pruning unused neural connections and strengthening important ones. This reorganization happens primarily during sleep. Chronic sleep restriction during adolescence has measurable effects on cognitive function, emotional regulation, physical performance, and even long-term brain structure.

### Part 5: Evaluating Nutrition Claims — Your BS Detector

The nutrition industry thrives on confusion. The more confused consumers are, the more supplements they buy, the more diet books they purchase, and the more "revolutionary" products they try. You are going to build a BS detector.

**Questions to ask about any nutrition claim:**

1. **Who is telling me this, and what are they selling?** A supplement company telling you that you need their supplement is not evidence. It is marketing. A fitness influencer promoting a product they are paid to promote is not a credible source.

2. **What is the evidence?** "Studies show" means nothing without specifics. Which study? How many people? Was it in humans or rats? Was it a randomized controlled trial or an observational study? Was the effect size meaningful or trivially small?

3. **Does it contradict well-established science?** If someone claims that a single food or supplement will dramatically transform your body, and this claim contradicts decades of established nutritional science, the claim is almost certainly wrong. Revolutionary discoveries in nutrition are rare. Revolutionary marketing claims are constant.

4. **Is the effect size meaningful?** A supplement might produce a "statistically significant" increase in performance that amounts to a 1% improvement detectable only in a laboratory. That is real but practically meaningless for you. Eating enough protein and sleeping well will produce a 20% improvement in your recovery and performance. Focus on the big levers first.

5. **The hierarchy of importance:** Calories > Macronutrients > Food quality > Meal timing > Supplements. Most people obsess about supplements (the smallest lever) while ignoring calories and protein (the biggest levers). Get the big things right before worrying about the small things.

### Part 6: Recovery — The Missing Piece

Training is a stress. Recovery is the adaptation. Without recovery, training is just damage.

**Rest days:** Your body needs days with reduced training load to consolidate adaptations. A minimum of one full rest day per week (two for very intense training blocks) is not laziness — it is programming.

**Active recovery:** Light movement on rest days — a walk, easy swimming, gentle mobility work — promotes blood flow without adding training stress. Active recovery is better than complete inactivity for most people.

**The overtraining myth:** Most people who think they are overtrained are actually under-recovered. The solution is usually not less training but more sleep, more food, and more rest days. True overtraining takes months of sustained excessive volume to produce. Under-recovery takes one bad week of sleep.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Macronutrient Understanding
**Developing well:** The student can look at a meal and estimate macronutrient composition. They understand the different roles of protein, carbohydrates, and fat. They can explain caloric balance in their own words.

**Developing slowly:** The student memorizes definitions but cannot apply them. They cannot estimate the protein content of a meal or explain why caloric balance matters.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student getting hands-on practice with food identification, or only reading about it? The food log exercise and regular mealtime discussion build practical understanding.

### 2. Sleep Practice
**Developing well:** The student follows sleep hygiene consistently, reports feeling rested, and can explain why sleep matters for physical development.

**Developing slowly:** The student resists bedtime, continues screen use before bed, and shows signs of chronic sleep debt (irritability, difficulty concentrating, excessive daytime sleepiness).

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the sleep environment optimized? Is screen cessation enforced? Is the schedule consistent? Address environmental factors before attributing sleep issues to the student's behavior.

### 3. Critical Evaluation Skills
**Developing well:** The student spontaneously questions nutrition claims. They ask "who is selling this?" and "what's the evidence?" when encountering ads or peer claims. They can analyze a supplement ad using the BS Detector framework.

**Developing slowly:** The student accepts claims uncritically or shows no interest in evaluating them. They repeat peer claims ("you need BCAAs for muscle growth") without examination.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you practicing the BS Detector together regularly? This skill develops through repeated application, not a single lesson. Find claims to evaluate together weekly.
